1: A direct connection: "Be merciful to me, O God, be merciful to me, for my soul takes refuge in you; in the shadow of your wings I will take shelter until the storms of destruction pass." The "shadow of your wings" as refuge is a Psalter image for divine protection (Psalms 17:8; Psalms 36:7; Psalms 61:4; Psalms 63:7; Psalms 91:4). Jesus applies the wings-image to himself in Matthew 23:37: "Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you, how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing." Christ is the one under whose wings shelter is found — and also the one whose longing to shelter was rejected.
2: A revelation of God: "I cry out to God Most High, to God who carries out his purpose for me." The divine purpose-fulfillment — YHWH ʾElyôn working out his plan for the Psalmist — is the providence that governs every apparent crisis. Acts 2:23: "This man was handed over to you by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge; and you, with the help of wicked men, put him to death by nailing him to the cross." The God who "carries out his purpose" for David's crisis is the same God whose deliberate plan governs the cross — what appears as defeat is the execution of divine purpose.
3: A revelation of God: "He will send from heaven and rescue me; he will rebuke the one who tramples me. God will send his steadfast love and his faithfulness." The divine sending from heaven of rescue — and of ḥesed and ʾĕmet (steadfast love and faithfulness) — is the Incarnation. John 3:17: "For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but to save the world through him." The rescue sent from heaven is the Son; the steadfast love and faithfulness that accompany it are the attributes of Christ himself — "full of grace and truth" (John 1:14, the NT equivalent of ḥesed wĕʾĕmet).
4: A shadow: "My soul is surrounded by lions; I must lie down among those who breathe fire — human beings whose teeth are spears and arrows and whose tongue is a sharp sword." The bestial imagery of dangerous enemies — lion-people with weapon-mouths — is the Psalm's vivid depiction of hostile power. 1 Peter 5:8: "Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour." The "lions" of Psalms 57:4 are the human instruments of the satanic opposition that Christ defeats (1 John 3:8: "The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work"). The tongue-as-sword imagery recurs in Revelation 1:16 (the sword from Christ's mouth).
5: A direct connection: "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" The refrain of vv5 and 11 is the doxological prayer for divine cosmic exaltation — YHWH's glory covering the whole earth. This is fulfilled in Christ's ascension and glorification: Philippians 2:9-11, "Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father." The exaltation above the heavens of Psalms 57:5 is Christ's ascension; the glory over all the earth is the universal Lordship he now holds.
6: A shadow: "They spread a net for my feet; my soul was beaten down; they dug a pit in my path, but they themselves have fallen into it." The reversal — the pit dug for the righteous becomes the trap of the wicked — is the Wisdom pattern of evil self-defeating. Acts 4:27-28: "Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel in this city to conspire against your holy servant Jesus... They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The pit they dug — the cross — became the instrument of their own defeat. 1 Corinthians 2:8: "None of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory."
7: A revelation of God: "My heart is fixed, O God, my heart is firm; I will sing and make melody!" The fixedness of the Psalmist's heart — unmovable, settled in trust despite the surrounding net and pit — is the foundation of praise even before rescue. Hebrews 12:2: Christ endured the cross "for the joy set before him" — the fixed heart of Psalms 57:7 is the same joy-grounded steadiness that Christ maintains through the Passion. The "singing and making melody" from a firm heart is the resurrection praise: Hebrews 2:12 shows Christ singing in the post-resurrection assembly (citing Psalms 22:22).
8: A theme: "Awake, my honor! Awake, harp and lyre! I will rouse the dawn!" The command to rouse the dawn — to summon morning before it comes, through anticipatory praise — is the posture of the one who trusts in resurrection before it arrives. Mark 16:2: "Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they were on their way to the tomb." The "rousing of the dawn" in Psalms 57:8 is the dawn of Easter — the first light of the resurrection morning that vindicates the trust of all who praised before the answer came.
9: A direct connection: "I will give you thanks, O Lord, among the peoples; I will sing your praise among the nations." Paul quotes this verse in Romans 15:9 as one of four OT texts demonstrating that Christ came to bring the Gentiles into Israel's praise: "Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles; I will sing hymns to your name" — citing it alongside Psalms 18:49, Deuteronomy 32:43, and Isaiah 11:10 to show that the Gentile mission was always in the OT script. The thanksgiving "among the nations" of Psalms 57:9 is the Messianic community's praise that the apostolic mission enacts in every people group.
10: A revelation of God: "For your steadfast love reaches to the heavens, your faithfulness to the skies." The cosmic reach of divine ḥesed — touching the heavens — is John 1:14's content: "full of grace and truth." The steadfast love and faithfulness that reach to the heavens have descended to earth in the Incarnation: the infinite ḥesed of YHWH has become personal in Jesus Christ. Ephesians 3:18-19: "to grasp how wide and long and high and deep is the love of Christ, and to know this love that surpasses knowledge." The heavens-reaching love of Psalms 57:10 is the love that surpasses knowledge in Christ.
11: A direct connection: "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" The closing refrain repeats v5 — the doxological petition that bookends the Psalm. The structural repetition underscores the centrality of divine exaltation as the goal and ground of all human experience — crisis, pit, net, and rescue are all subsumed under the cosmic desire for YHWH's glory. Christ's ascension ("he was taken up before their very eyes, and a cloud hid him from their sight," Acts 1:9) and present session ("he sat down at the right hand of the throne of God," Hebrews 12:2) are the fulfillment: exalted above the heavens, his glory over all the earth.
